tips.title: Right-size the model
tips.body: The single biggest lever. Prices differ 50x or more between flagship and budget tiers, and most everyday tasks don't need a frontier model. Try one tier down — if quality holds, keep going down until it doesn't.

tips.title: Cache your prompt prefix
tips.body: Most providers offer prompt caching: repeated content (system prompts, docs, conversation history) is billed at ~10% of the normal input price. Keep stable content at the start of the prompt and volatile content (timestamps, per-request IDs) at the end — one changed byte early in the prompt invalidates everything after it.

tips.title: Compress your prompts
tips.body: Politeness padding, hedging, and restating context all cost tokens on every single call. Say what you want directly — the model doesn't need warming up.

example.before: I was wondering if you could possibly help me out by taking a look at the following text and providing me with a brief summary of the main points contained within it?

tips.title: Send only the context you need
tips.body: Don't paste a whole file when the function is enough, or a whole PDF when one section answers the question. Input tokens are billed on every request — including everything the model didn't need.

tips.title: Remember: output costs ~5x input
tips.body: Output tokens are typically 5–6x the price of input tokens. Ask for concise answers, set a max output limit, and skip the 'explain your reasoning' unless you'll actually read it.

example.before: Explain in detail, with examples, all the ways I could fix this bug.
example.after: Give the single best fix for this bug, in one short paragraph.

tips.title: Use batch APIs for non-urgent work
tips.body: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google all offer batch endpoints at ~50% off in exchange for results within hours instead of seconds. Perfect for nightly jobs, backfills, and bulk labeling.

tips.title: Ask for structure, not prose
tips.body: If code will consume the answer, request JSON or a fixed format (most APIs can enforce a schema). Structured answers are shorter than prose, and you skip a second 'now extract the data' call.

tips.title: Turn the reasoning dial down
tips.body: Reasoning models let you control thinking depth (effort levels, thinking budgets). High effort on a simple task burns tokens on deliberation you don't need — reserve deep thinking for problems that deserve it.

tips.title: Draft cheap, polish expensive
tips.body: Use a budget model for the messy exploratory phase — brainstorming, first drafts, bulk generation — then hand only the survivors to a frontier model for the final pass.

tips.title: Audit your system prompt
tips.body: Every request pays for the whole system prompt. If it's grown to thousands of tokens of rules and examples, prune it: remove instructions the model already follows and examples that stopped earning their keep.

tips.title: Don't resend the whole conversation
tips.body: Chat APIs are stateless — you resend the history each turn, so a long conversation gets more expensive every message. Summarize older turns, or start a fresh conversation once the useful context fits in a paragraph.

tips.title: Use the minimum few-shot examples
tips.body: One well-chosen example often teaches as much as five. Each example rides along on every request, so test how few you can get away with.

tips.title: Retrieve, don't stuff
tips.body: Instead of putting your entire knowledge base in the prompt, retrieve the handful of relevant chunks per question (RAG). The model reads 2K tokens instead of 200K — and usually answers better, too.

tips.title: Say 'answer only' when you mean it
tips.body: Models love a preamble ('Great question! Let's break this down…'). For lookups and quick facts, ask for the answer only — you'll cut output tokens and read faster.

example.after: What's the capital of Australia? Answer with just the city name.
example.savings: one word instead of a paragraph
